review_summary: The transcript is clear enough to create questions about InstructGPT, ChatGPT, the practical role of DPO in open-source and production settings, and remaining risks such as reward hacking or hallucination after RLHF.

repaired_question_bank.question: What does InstructGPT represent in the history of post-training?
choices: It is the model family that helped define the SFT-plus-RLHF pipeline for instruction-following assistants.
It is the first model to use DPO instead of all other post-training steps.
It is a version of GPT-3 that used only prompt engineering and no fine-tuning.
It is a dialogue evaluation benchmark that replaced MMLU.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: InstructGPT is presented as a milestone that established the modern post-training workflow: instruction tuning, reward modeling, and reinforcement learning to make models respond better to user intent.

transcript_quotes: InstructGPT is presented as the first model family defining the SFT plus

repaired_question_bank.question: What difference is emphasized between ChatGPT and InstructGPT?
choices: ChatGPT uses the same core ideas but is better optimized for dialogue and user-interaction data.
ChatGPT removes post-training entirely and relies only on large-scale pretraining.
ChatGPT does not use high-quality data because scale is sufficient.
ChatGPT is only a benchmark, not an assistant system.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: The core algorithmic ideas behind ChatGPT and InstructGPT are similar, but data quality and optimization for conversational contexts are practically important for producing the ChatGPT experience.

transcript_quotes: ChatGPT uses the same core algorithmic ideas but is optimized for dialogue.

repaired_question_bank.question: What warning about preference optimization is emphasized at the end of the lecture?
choices: A model can become more persuasive or verbose while still being untruthful, and RLHF does not eliminate hallucinations.
Once RLHF or DPO is used, reward hacking and hallucination disappear completely.
Humans always prefer shorter and more accurate answers over longer ones, so there is no objective mismatch.
Open-source models cannot use DPO, so they do not face post-training risks.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: Optimizing for human preference is not the same as optimizing for truth. If annotators prefer confident, complete, or longer answers, the model may learn those signals without becoming more accurate.
